title: Task 3: Add explicit top-level anomaly classes

turns.speaker: HOST_A
turns.text: Task 3 sounds simple on paper: Align the dataset output with the Phase-0 anomaly taxonomy while keeping the current rule-level labels.

turns.speaker: HOST_B
turns.text: And the reason it matters is straightforward: Phase 0 framed the problem in top-level classes: - task failure - process inefficiency - unwarranted continuation

turns.speaker: HOST_A
turns.text: What I like here is that the implementation does not chase a grand redesign. It tightens one seam in the pipeline.

turns.speaker: HOST_B
turns.text: Exactly. The implemented result for Task 3 is now marked done in the plan, with concrete verification commands recorded for the repo.

turns.speaker: HOST_A
turns.text: [gasp] So what changes downstream because Task 3 is already implemented?

turns.speaker: HOST_B
turns.text: Explicit anomaly classes make later reporting, stratified evaluation, and curriculum choices far easier.

turns.speaker: HOST_A
turns.text: That means the value is not just local correctness. It is leverage for everything that comes after.

turns.speaker: HOST_B
turns.text: Once class labels exist, coherence screening and rule tuning can be analyzed at both rule-level and class-level granularity.

turns.speaker: HOST_A
turns.text: [chuckle] This is very home-lab coded: small patch, big increase in trust.

turns.speaker: HOST_B
turns.text: Yes. It follows the same discipline across the whole plan: keep the architecture modest, but make the data and labels harder to fool.

turns.speaker: HOST_A
turns.text: And because Task 3 is complete, the listener can treat this as a concrete repo change rather than a hypothetical roadmap item.

turns.speaker: HOST_B
turns.text: Exactly. This episode is not selling a future intention. It is documenting an implemented improvement and the next layer of value it unlocks.
